entries.openingLines: Mr and Mrs Brown first met Paddington on a railway platform. In fact, that was how he came to have such an unusual name for a bear, for Paddington was the name of the station.
entries.title: Paddington
entries.author: Michael Bond

entries.openingLines: "There was once a velveteen rabbit, and in the beginning he was really splendid. He was fat and bunchy, as a rabbit should be; his coat was spotted brown and white, he had real thread whiskers, and his ears were lined with pink sateen."
entries.title: The Velveteen Rabbit
entries.author: Margery Williams

entries.openingLines: "It was in a space in a thicket that he came into the world, in one of those little, hidden places in the wood which seem to be open on every side but which in fact are completely surrounded by foliage."
entries.title: Bambi
entries.author: Felix Salten

entries.openingLines: The Mole had been working very hard all the morning, spring-cleaning his little home.
entries.title: The Wind in the Willows
entries.author: Kenneth Grahame

entries.openingLines: There was once upon a time a gentleman who married, for his second wife, the proudest and most haughty woman that was ever seen.
entries.title: Cinderella
entries.author: Charles Perrault

entries.openingLines: "I often have wondered about the Strat, have not you?"
entries.title: The Wizard of Oz
entries.author: L. Frank Baum

entries.openingLines: Once upon a time there was a piece of wood.
entries.title: Pinocchio
entries.author: Carlo Collodi

entries.openingLines: It was seven o'clock of a very warm evening in the Seeonee hills when Father Wolf woke up from his day's rest, scratched himself, yawned, and spread out his paws one after the other to get rid of the sleepy feeling in their tips.
entries.title: The Jungle Book
entries.author: Rudyard Kipling
